elements.sent-2411-001: Line segment detection is a fundamental low-level task in computer vision, and improvements in this task can impact more advanced methods that depend on it.
elements.sent-2411-002: Most new methods developed for line segment detection are based on Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs).
elements.sent-2411-003: Our paper seeks to address challenges that prevent the wider adoption of transformer-based methods for line segment detection.
elements.sent-2411-004: More specifically, we introduce a new model called Deformable Transformer-based Line Segment Detection (DT-LSD) that supports cross-scale interactions and can be trained quickly.
elements.sent-2411-005: This work proposes a novel Deformable Transformer-based Line Segment Detector (DT-LSD) that addresses LETR's drawbacks.
elements.sent-2411-006: For faster training, we introduce Line Contrastive DeNoising (LCDN), a technique that stabilizes the one-to-one matching process and speeds up training by 34$\times$.
elements.sent-2411-007: We show that DT-LSD is faster and more accurate than its predecessor transformer-based model (LETR) and outperforms all CNN-based models in terms of accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-008: In the Wireframe dataset, DT-LSD achieves 71.7 for $sAP^{10}$ and 73.9 for $sAP^{15}$; while 33.2 for $sAP^{10}$ and 35.1 for $sAP^{15}$ in the YorkUrban dataset.
elements.figure-2411-001: \linewidth{!}{ \begin{tabular}{ccc} \phantom{a}\includegraphics[width=0.9\linewidth]{figures/intro/feat_map_processing_convnets}\phantom{a} \phantom{a}\includegraphics[width=0.9\linewidth]{figures/intro/feat_map_processing_letr}\phantom{a} \phantom{a}\includegraphics[width=0.9\linewidth]{figures/intro/feat_map_processing_dtlsd}\phantom{a} \end{tabular}} \linewidth{!}{ \begin{tabular}{ccc} \phantom{hello}a) Feature Pyramid Network\phantom{hello} \phantom{hello}b) Global Attention Encoder\phantom{hello} \phantom{hello}c) Deformable Attention Encoder\phantom{hello} \end{tabular}} \caption{Feature map enhancing. All line segment detectors use a hierarchical backbone, but they differ from each other in their enhancing method. (a) CNN-based models use a feature pyramid network to combine two contiguous feature maps, allowing the propagation of global information to low-dimensional feature maps. However, no intra-scale interaction is applied to any feature map. (b) LETR \cite{LETR} uses a global attention encoder for each processed feature map, promoting the intra-scale interaction but not the cross-scale interaction since no information is passed between the two processed feature maps. (c) DT-LSD allows intra- and cross-scale (more than two feature maps) interactions by applying a deformable-attention encoder.} \label{fig: overview}
elements.sent-2411-010: Line segment detection is a low-level vision task used for higher-level tasks such as 3D reconstruction, camera calibration, vanishing point estimation, and scene understanding.
elements.sent-2411-011: Despite its importance, this problem remains open.
elements.sent-2411-012: Additionally, unlike other computer vision tasks (\eg object detection, 3d-estimation, camera calibration), transformer-based models are not popular to tackle this challenge, LinE segment TRansformers (LETR) \cite{LETR is the only transformer model for line segment detection in the literature.}
elements.sent-2411-013: Most recent methods \cite{hawp, lcnn, mlnet, sacwp, HAWPv2 uses Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) despite the fact that CNNs require a post-processing step to get the final predictions.}
elements.sent-2411-014: All models have a backbone that produces a set of hierarchical feature maps for further processing as shown in \cref{fig: overview .}
elements.sent-2411-015: CNN-based models use a feature pyramid network (FPN) as an enhancing method following the HourglassNet method \cite{hourglass (see \cref{fig: overview a).}}
elements.sent-2411-016: This method demonstrates the importance of cross-scale interaction since the new feature map is computed from contiguous feature maps, allowing the propagation of the global information from the highest-level feature map to the lower-level ones.
elements.sent-2411-017: On the other hand, LETR produces an enhanced feature map using a single feature map, as depicted in \cref{fig: overview b.}
elements.sent-2411-018: LETR demonstrates the ability of the global attention mechanism \cite{attention to capture long-term relationships between the pixels of the same feature map (intra-scale processing), providing rich features.}
elements.sent-2411-019: This paper develops a new transformer-based models for line segment detection.
elements.sent-2411-020: First, we improve LETR's feature map-enhancing method.
elements.sent-2411-021: We choose the deformable attention mechanism \cite{deformable-DETR for its ability to combine both intra- and cross-scale processing.}
elements.sent-2411-022: We illustrate our idea in \cref{fig: overview c, where a deformable-attention encoder is used for feature map enhancement.}
elements.sent-2411-023: The encoder receives a set of hierarchical feature maps\footnote{feature maps are pre-processed by a $1\times1\text{\phantom{a }\mathrm{conv}$ to assure all the inputs have the same amount of channels.}}
elements.sent-2411-024: Second, we reduce the number of epochs required for training.
elements.sent-2411-025: Inspired from \cite{dn-DETR, dino , we propose Line Contrastive DeNoising (LCDN) as training technique to to accelerate the convergence of the training process.}
elements.sent-2411-026: We show the efficiency of LCDN in \Cref{tab:dn_components where we improve the metrics while keeping the same amount of epochs.}
elements.sent-2411-027: Our contributions are summarized as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-028: We propose a novel end-to-end transformer-based framework showing that outperforms CNN-based line segment detectors.
elements.sent-2411-029: This is achieved by using the deformable attention mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-030: We introduce a highly-efficient training technique, Line Contrastive DeNoising, to reduce the number of epochs.
elements.sent-2411-031: This technique allows DT-LSD to achieve convergence in a similar number of epochs to CNN-based models.
elements.sent-2411-032: On two datasets (Wireframe \cite{wireframe_data and YorkUrban \cite{york_data ), our end-to-end transformer-based model present a performance improvement over state-of-the-art methods on both structural and heat map metrics.}}
elements.sent-2411-033: Our work opens up opportunities for line segment detectors to remove hand-crafted post-processing by utilizing end-to-end transformer-based models.
elements.sent-2411-034: In what remains of this paper, we describe previous state-of-the-art methods in line segment detection, as well as the two attention mechanisms in \cref{sec: background .}
elements.sent-2411-035: Next, we describe the methodology for DT-LSD in \cref{sec: methodology .}
elements.sent-2411-036: Then, we provide information about model parameters and training settings, comparison against previous state-of-the-art models, and the ablation studies of DT-LSD in \cref{sec: results .}
elements.sent-2411-037: Finally, we summarize our findings in \cref{sec: conclusion .}
elements.sent-2411-038: The Hough Transform (HT) \cite{hough remains an important method for line detection.}
elements.sent-2411-039: First, the Hough Transform applies Canny edge detection \cite{canny to obtain line segment candidates.}
elements.sent-2411-040: Candidate lines are represented in polar form.
elements.sent-2411-041: Here, we note that candidate lines are evaluated based on the overlapping number of pixels between the lines and the detected edges.
elements.sent-2411-042: Variations include the use of the Radon Transform and the Revoting Hough Transform.
elements.sent-2411-043: In regions dominated by a large density of edges, the HT can generate a large number of false positives.
elements.sent-2411-044: Grompone von Gioi \etal proposed a linear-time Line Segment Detector (LSD) \cite{LSD to address this problem.}
elements.sent-2411-045: LSD uses line-supported regions and line segment validation.
elements.sent-2411-046: The approach also reduced time complexity through the use of a pseudo-sorting algorithm based on gradient magnitudes.
elements.sent-2411-047: A fundamental advantage of traditional methods is that they do not require training for specific datasets.
elements.sent-2411-048: Learning-based line segment detectors have shown significant improvements compared to traditional approaches.
elements.sent-2411-049: The methods include different approaches that focus on line junctions, attraction field maps (AFM), transformers, and combining traditional approaches with deep learning techniques.
elements.sent-2411-050: The Holistically-Attracted Wireframe Parser (HAWP) \cite{hawp proposed a 4-dimensional attraction field map, and later HAWPv2 \cite{HAWPv2 , a hybrid model of HAWP and self-supervision, was introduced.}}
elements.sent-2411-051: MLNet \cite{mlnet and SACWP \cite{sacwp incorporated cross-scale feature interaction on HAWP model.}}
elements.sent-2411-052: In \cite{afm,lcnn , the authors developed a method for detecting line junctions which were used to provide candidate line segments.}
elements.sent-2411-053: Then, a classifier validated the candidates and produced the final set of predicted line segments.
elements.sent-2411-054: LSDNet [18] used a CNN model to generate an angle field and line mask that were used to detect line segments using the LSD method.
elements.sent-2411-055: HT-HAWP and HT-LCNN \cite{lin2020ht added global geometric line priors through the Hough Transform in deep learning networks to address the lack of labeled data.}
elements.sent-2411-056: However, the above methods requires of post-processing steps to produce the final output.
elements.sent-2411-057: In contrast, Line segment transformers (LETR) \cite{LETR remove post-processing steps by using an end-to-end transformer-based model that relies on a coarse-to-fine strategy with two encoder-decoder transformer blocks.}
elements.sent-2411-058: One crucial factor of LETR's slow convergence is the global attention mechanism, which only does intra-scale feature processing.
elements.sent-2411-059: Plus, the global attention leads to very high computational complexity.
elements.sent-2411-060: To understand the complexity requirements of LETR, we revisit the global attention mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-061: We define global attention using \cite{attention : \begin{equation}\mathrm{GlobAtt}(Q, K, V) = \mathrm{softmax}\big(\frac{QK^T}{\sqrt{d}}\big)V \label{eq:glob_attn}\end{equation} where $Q, K, V, \text{ and d$ represent the queries, keys, values and the hidden dimensions, respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-062: For object and line segment detection, we define $K=V \in \mathbb{R ^{HW\times d}$ as the flattened form of the feature map $f\in \mathbb{R ^{H\times W\times d}$ where $H$ and $W$ are the height and width, respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-063: In the encoder, we have $Q=K=V$ resulting in a complexity time of $O(H^2W^2d)$.
elements.sent-2411-064: Similarly, for the decoder, we have $Q\in \mathbb{R ^{N\times d}$ where $N$ is the number of queries producing a complexity time of $O(HWC^2 + NHWC)$.}
elements.sent-2411-065: Based on our previous discussion, it is clear that the bottleneck of transformer-based models is the encoder, whose complexity time quadratically increases with respect to the spatial size of the feature map.
elements.sent-2411-066: To address this issue, Zhu \etal \cite{deformable-DETR proposed the deformable attention mechanism, inspired by deformable convolution \cite{deform_conv .}}
elements.sent-2411-067: Unlike global attention, the deformable attention module only attends to a fixed number $k$ of keys for each query (see Fig. 2 from \cite{deformable-DETR for a visual representation).}
elements.sent-2411-068: Given an input feature map ${f \in \mathbb{R ^{H\times W\times d}$, let $q$ be the index of a query element with content feature $z_q$ and a 2d-reference point $p_q$, the deformable attention for one attention head\footnote{The multi-head deformable attention equation is in page 5 section 4.1 in \cite{deformable-DETR is mathematically defined as \begin{equation}\mathrm{DeformAttn}(z_q, p_q, x) = \sum_{i=1}^{k} A_{qi} \cdot f(p_q + \Delta p_{qi}) \label{eq:deform_attn}\end{equation} where $i$ indexes the sampling keys and $k$ is the total sampling keys number ($k\ll HW$).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-069: The $i^{th}$ sampling key is computed as $p_q + \Delta p_{qi}$ where $\Delta p_{qi}$ is the sampling offset.
elements.sent-2411-071: Comparing \cref{eq:glob_attn to \cref{eq:deform_attn , $\mathrm{softmax}\big(QK^T/\sqrt{d}\big)$ is replaced by $A_q$, and $V$ by $x(p_q + \Delta p_{qi )$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-072: So, the complexity time for the deformable encoder is $O(HWd^2)$, which has a linear complexity.
elements.sent-2411-073: For the deformable decoder, the complexity time is $O(kNd^2)$ where $N$ is the total number of queries, and the spatial dimensions of $x$ are irrelevant.
elements.sent-2411-074: Apart from reducing the memory and time complexities, deformable attention has a variation called multi-scale deformable attention ($\mathrm{MSDeformAttn (z_q, \hat{p _q, \{f_l\}_{l=1 ^L)$) that allows cross-scale feature interaction and is defined as \begin{equation}\sum_{l=1}^{L}\sum_{i=1}^{k}A_{lqi}\cdot f_l(\phi_l(\hat{p}_q) + \Delta p_{lqi}) \label{eq:multi_deform_attn}\end{equation} where $\{f_l\}_{l=1 ^L$ is a set of $L$ multi-scale feature maps, and $f_l\in \mathbb{R ^{H_l\times W_l \times d}$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-075: The normalized 2d coordinates $\hat{p _q$ has its values lying in the range of $[0, 1]$, and is re-scaled to dimensions of the $l^{\text{th }$-level feature map by the function $\phi_l$.}}
elements.sent-2411-076: Like \cref{eq:deform_attn , the attention weight $A_{lqi}$ satisfies $\sum_{l=1 ^{L}\sum_{i=1 ^{k A_{lqi = 1$.}}}}}
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=0.9\textwidth]{figures/algorithm/model} \caption{Framework of the proposed DT-LSD model. DT-LSD uses a deformable encoder and deformable decoder layers. Furthermore, it uses a set of mixed queries as a training strategy which does not influence the inference time.} \label{fig: model}
elements.sent-2411-077: We present the architecture of DT-LSD, an end-to-end deformable transformer for line segment detection, in \cref{fig: model .}
elements.sent-2411-078: First, we pass an RGB image to a backbone to produce a set of hierarchical feature maps.
elements.sent-2411-079: Second, a deformable encoder enhances the backbone's feature maps.
elements.sent-2411-080: Third, we apply query selection to choose the top-K queries \footnote{In the encoder, feature maps pixels are treated as queries as the initial 4D dynamic line endpoints.}
elements.sent-2411-081: Fourth, we feed the initial dynamic line endpoints and the static (learnable) content queries to the deformable decoder to promote the interaction between queries and the enhanced feature maps.
elements.sent-2411-082: Fifth, two independent multi-layer perceptron networks process the decoder's output queries to estimate the line segment endpoints and classify whether a query contains a line.
elements.sent-2411-083: For the training process, we added an extra branch to perform line contrastive denoising, which did not affect the inference time.
elements.sent-2411-084: In this section, we do not describe the decoder and the one-to-one matching since they are already described in \cite{deformable-DETR and \cite{LETR .}}
elements.sent-2411-085: The encoder is a fundamental part of our network since it enhances the backbone's feature maps.
elements.sent-2411-086: However, these feature maps do not have any dimensions in common.
elements.sent-2411-087: For this reason, it is important to pre-process them before passing them to the encoder.
elements.sent-2411-088: As shown in \cref{fig:feat_pre_proc , given an RGB image of dimensions $(H, W, 3)$, the backbone produces a set of hierarchical feature maps $\{f_l\}_{l=1 ^5$ where $f_l\in \mathbb{R ^{H_l\times W_l \times d_i}$ and $H_l = H/2^{l+1}$ and $W_l = W/2^{l+1}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-089: Since $\{f_l\}_{l=1 ^5$ do not have any dimension in common and we do not want to lose spatial resolution, we apply a $1\times1$ convolution to each feature so that the whole set has the same number of channels $\{f'_l\}_{l=2 ^5$ where $f'_l\in \mathbb{R ^{H_l\times W_l \times 256}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-090: Next, we flatten their spatial dimension, followed by stacking them together and adding the position encoding ($PE$), creating the vector \begin{equation}\hat{F} = \mathrm{stack}(\hat{f}_2, \hat{f}_3, \hat{f}_4, \hat{f}_5)\end{equation} where $\hat{f _i = \mathrm{flatten (f'_i + PE(f'_i))$ and $\hat{F}\in\mathbb{R ^{L \times 256}$ \footnote{The $\mathrm{stack}$ and $\mathrm{flatten}$ functions are associative functions.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-091: We pass $\hat{F}$ to the encoder where each of its stacked pixels is treated as a query $q \in \mathbb{R ^{1\times256}$.}
elements.sent-2411-092: For each query, we produce a fixed number $k=4$ of offsets per feature map followed by applying \cref{eq:multi_deform_attn .}
elements.sent-2411-093: In general, we compute a total of 16 offsets per attention head, where 4 of them are for intra-scale interaction, and the other 12 are for cross-scale interaction.
elements.sent-2411-094: Besides combining both types of interactions, we address the global attention's time complexity and the convolutional layers' kernel space restriction.
elements.sent-2411-095: A problem for end-to-end transformer-based methods is the one-to-one matching technique, which removes the need for non-maximum suppression (NMS) but is unstable in matching queries with ground truth.
elements.sent-2411-096: The main difference between one-to-one matching and NMS is that the first one uses scores to do the matching.
elements.sent-2411-097: In contrast, the second one eliminates candidates depending on how much the bounding boxes of the candidates belonging to the same class overlap.
elements.sent-2411-098: In this section, we present Line Contrastive Denoising (LCDN), a training technique for stabilizing the matching process inspired from \cite{dino .}
elements.sent-2411-099: While LCDN is used to speed up training, it is not part of the final inference model.
elements.sent-2411-100: LCDN facilitates the matching by teaching the Hungarian Matcher to accept queries whose predicted line's endpoints lie on or are close to a ground-truth line and to reject queries whose predicted line's endpoints are far away from the ground-truth line.
elements.sent-2411-101: To achieve this, we create positive and negative queries by performing line length scaling and line rotation.
elements.sent-2411-102: The length scaling consists of varying the length of the line segment, with original length ${l}$, such that positive queries have a length in a range of $[0, l]$ and the negative queries $(l, 2l)$.
elements.sent-2411-103: For line rotation, we rotate the line in a range of $(-\tau, \tau)$ for positive, where $\tau$ is the fixed angle.
elements.sent-2411-104: For negative queries, the rotation is in the $(-2\tau, -\tau] \cup [\tau, 2\tau)$ range.
elements.sent-2411-105: We present an example of our LCDN technique in \cref{fig: lcdn b and a comparison against the Contrastive DeNoising (CDN) \cite{dino in \Cref{tab:dn_components .}}}
elements.sent-2411-106: Since LCDN generates extra groups of denoising queries from ground-truth lines, this can harm the training process if the prediction queries interact with the denoising queries.
elements.sent-2411-107: The denoising queries contain ground-truth information, so if a matching query sees this information, it will start training with information that should not be known.
elements.sent-2411-108: In the other case, we want the denoising queries to see the information stored by the matching queries.
elements.sent-2411-109: We manually implement this by using an attention mask.
elements.sent-2411-110: Note that only queries from the same denoising group are allowed to interact with each other, but all denoising groups interact with the matching group.
elements.figure-2411-004: 0.98\linewidth{!}{ \begin{tabular}{ccc} \includegraphics[width=0.95\columnwidth]{figures/algorithm/CDN} \phantom{aaa} \includegraphics[width=0.95\columnwidth]{figures/algorithm/LCDN} \end{tabular} } 0.98\linewidth{!}{ \begin{tabular}{ccc} \phantom{hello}a) CDN (DINO)\phantom{hello} \phantom{aaa} \phantom{hello}b) LCDN (ours)\phantom{hello} \end{tabular} } \newcommand{\crule}[3][red]{\textcolor{#1}{\rule{#2}{#3} \rule{#2}{#3} \rule{#2}{#3}}} 0.98\linewidth{!}{ \begin{tabular}{ccc} \crule[green]{0.01\linewidth}{0.01\linewidth} Ground Truth \crule[blue]{0.01\linewidth}{0.01\linewidth} Positive query \crule[red]{0.01\linewidth}{0.01\linewidth} Negative query \end{tabular} } \caption{Comparison between contrastive denoising techniques applied to line segments. We present two different line segments and their positive and negative queries. We use solid and dashed to different between line segment samples.} \label{fig: lcdn}
elements.sent-2411-111: We choose the focal loss \cite{focal_loss for line classification because it can deal with class imbalance.}
elements.sent-2411-112: The focal loss encourages training on uncertain samples by penalizing samples with predicted probability $\hat{p}$ that is away from 0 or 1 as given by (per sample loss): \begin{equation}\mathcal{L}_{\text{class}}^{(i)} = -(\alpha_1(1-\hat{p}^{{(i)}})^\gamma\log \hat{p}^{(i)}+\alpha_2 (\hat{p}^{{(i)}})^\gamma\log (1-\hat{p}^{{(i)}}))\end{equation} with $\alpha_1=1$, $\alpha_2=0.25$, and $\gamma=2$.
elements.sent-2411-113: For each line candidate $\hat{\mathbf{l }$, we use the $L_1$ loss to compute the distance from the ground truth points.}
elements.sent-2411-115: If the classifier accepts the line candidate, we get $c_i=1$ for the classification output.. Else, $c_i=0$.
elements.sent-2411-116: Let $\mathbf{l ^{(i) _j$ denote the $j^{th}$ endpoint component of the $i$-th ground-trtuh line.}}
elements.sent-2411-117: We have four endpoint components because we use two coordinate points representing the line segment.
elements.sent-2411-118: The $\mathcal{L _{\text{line }$ loss function is then given by: \begin{equation}\mathcal{L}_{\text{line}}^{(i)} = \mathds{1}_{\{c_i \neq \emptyset\}}\sum_{j=1}^{4} |\mathbf{l}_j^{{(i)}} - \hat{\mathbf{l}}^{(i)}_j|.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-119: The final loss $\mathcal{L}$ is a linear combination of the two loss functions: \begin{equation}\mathcal{L} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \lambda_{\text{cls}} \, \mathcal{L}_{\text{class}} ^{(i)}+ \lambda_{\text{line}}\, \mathcal{L}_{\text{line}} ^{(i)}\end{equation} where $\lambda_{\text{class =2$, $\lambda_{\text{line =5$, and $N$ is the total number of instances.}}}}
elements.figure-2411-005: 0.65\columnwidth{!}{ \begin{tabular}{l|l} Parameter Value number of feature maps 4 number of encoder layers 6 encoder sampling points 4 number of decoder layers 6 decoder sampling points 4 hidden dim 256 feedforward dim 1024 number of heads 8 number of classes 2 number of queries 900 denoising number 300 label noise ratio 0.5 line scaling 1.0 line rotation 7$^{\circ}$ line loss weight 5 class loss weight 2 optimizer AdamW initial learning rate 1e-4 initial learning rate of backbone 1e-5 weight decay 1e-4 batch size 2 total number of epochs 24 learning rate drop 21 \end{tabular}} \caption{DT-LSD architecture parameters and training setup.} \label{tab: hyperparameters}
elements.sent-2411-121: We used the ShangaiTech Wireframe dataset for comparisons \cite{wireframe_data .}
elements.sent-2411-122: The dataset consists of 5462 (indoor and outdoor) images of hand-made environments.
elements.sent-2411-123: The ground-truth line segments were manually labeled.
elements.sent-2411-124: The goal of the dataset was to provide line segments with meaningful geometric information about the scene.
elements.sent-2411-125: We split the dataset into 5000 images for training and 462 for testing.. York Urban Dataset.
elements.sent-2411-126: We also use the York Urban dataset \cite{york_data .}
elements.sent-2411-127: The dataset consists of 122 (45 indoor and 57 outdoor) images of size $640 \times 480$ pixels.
elements.sent-2411-128: Denis \etal generated ground-truth line segments using an interactive MATLAB program with a sub-pixel precision.
elements.sent-2411-129: We only used this dataset for testing.
elements.sent-2411-130: For the results shown in \cref{sec: sota_comparison , we follow \cite{dab-DETR, detr, deformable-DETR, dino, dn-DETR and apply data augmentations to the training set.}}
elements.sent-2411-131: During training, we reprise the input images such that the shortest side is at least 480 and at most 800 pixels while the longest at most 1333.
elements.sent-2411-132: At the evaluation stage, we resize the image with the shortest side at least 640 pixels.

Based on the models trained on the Wireframe dataset, we provide test results on the both YorkUrban and Wireframe dataset. The best results are given in boldface. Underlines are used for the $\underline{\text{second best}}$. } \label{tab: results}
elements.figure-2411-007: \begin{tabular}{cccc} \includegraphics[width=0.48\columnwidth]{figures/results/sAP_curve_wireframe} \includegraphics[width=0.48\columnwidth]{figures/results/sAP_curve_york} \includegraphics[width=0.48\columnwidth]{figures/results/aph_curve_wireframe} \includegraphics[width=0.48\columnwidth]{figures/results/aph_curve_york} \end{tabular} \caption{Precision-Recall (PR) curves. PR curves comparisons between L-CNN\cite{lcnn}, LETR\cite{LETR} and DT-LSD(ours) using sAP$^{10}$ and AP$^\text{H}$ metrics for Wireframe and YorkUrban datasets.} \label{fig: pr_curve}
elements.sent-2411-133: We use the SwinL \cite{liu2021Swin as a backbone for our deformable encoder-decoder transformer model.}
elements.sent-2411-134: For the deformable transformer, we followed the recommendation of DINO \cite{dino .}
elements.sent-2411-135: We used 4 sampling offsets for the encoder and decoder, 900 queries to predict line segments, 6 stacked-encoder layers, and 6 stacked-decoder layers.
elements.sent-2411-136: We summarize the DT-LSD architecture parameters and training parameters in \Cref{tab: hyperparameters .}
elements.sent-2411-137: We train DT-LSD on a single Nvidia RTX A5500 GPU with a batch size of 2.
elements.sent-2411-138: We compare DT-LSD to many state-of-the-art models in \Cref{tab: results .}
elements.sent-2411-139: Our approach gives the most accurate result in all of our comparisons providing new state-of-the-art results in both datasets.
elements.sent-2411-140: In the Wireframe dataset, DT-LSD performs better than SCAWP (the second best result) by around 2 points in the sAP metric.
elements.sent-2411-141: In the YorkUrban dataset, DT-LSD outperforms MLNET (the second best result) by around 1 percentage point in the sAP metric and 1.6 in the AP$^\text{H}$.
elements.sent-2411-142: Comparing to LETR, we show a significant gain in all metrics.
elements.sent-2411-143: Furthermore, we note that DT-LSD is trained with just 24 epochs, significantly faster than the 825 epochs required for LETR.
elements.sent-2411-144: At the same time, DT-LSD runs at nearly 9 frames per second.
elements.sent-2411-145: We show the Precision-Recall (PR) curves for sAP$^{10}$ and AP$^\text{H}$ in \cref{fig: pr_curve .}
elements.sent-2411-146: DT-LSD has low performance for low recall values but surpasses LETR and L-CNN for recall values greater than 0.1.
elements.sent-2411-147: We also provide qualitative comparative results in \cref{fig: outputs .}
elements.sent-2411-148: The images clearly show that the transformer-based approaches (LETR and DT-LSD) perform significantly better than LCNN and HAWP.
elements.sent-2411-149: Upon closer inspection, we can see that DT-LSD avoids noisy line detections that appear in LETR (\eg, inspect the center regions of third-row images).
elements.figure-2411-008: 0.98\linewidth{!}{ \begin{tabular}{ccccc} \includegraphics[width=0.25\linewidth]{figures/results/wireframe_vis/lcnn/00035228_VIS} \includegraphics[width=0.25\linewidth]{figures/results/wireframe_vis/hawp/00035228_VIS} \includegraphics[width=0.25\linewidth]{figures/results/wireframe_vis/LETR/00035228_VIS} \includegraphics[width=0.25\linewidth]{figures/results/wireframe_vis/MARRON/00035228_VIS} \includegraphics[width=0.25\linewidth]{figures/results/wireframe_vis/ground_truth/00035228_VIS} \includegraphics[width=0.25\linewidth]{figures/results/york_vis/lcnn/P1080116_VIS} \includegraphics[width=0.25\linewidth]{figures/results/york_vis/hawp/P1080116_VIS} \includegraphics[width=0.25\linewidth]{figures/results/york_vis/LETR/P1080116_VIS} \includegraphics[width=0.25\linewidth]{figures/results/york_vis/MARRON/P1080116_VIS} \includegraphics[width=0.25\linewidth]{figures/results/york_vis/ground_truth/P1080116_VIS} LCNN \cite{lcnn} HAWP \cite{hawp} LETR \cite{LETR} DT-LSD (ours) Ground-Truth \end{tabular} } \caption{Qualitative comparisons for line detection methods. From left to right: the columns provide example results from LCNN, HAWP, LETR, DT-LSD (ours) and the ground-truth. The top and bottom rows provide examples from the Wireframe and YorkUrban test sets, respectively. The bottom two rows provide examples from the YorkUrban test set.} \label{fig: outputs}
elements.sent-2411-150: Our comparisons are based on the number of epochs and the sAP metric as summarized in \Cref{tab:dn_components .}
elements.sent-2411-151: For a fair comparison, we train all the DINO\footnote{We use DINO-ResNet50-4scale from \cite{dino .}}
elements.sent-2411-152: Compared to the 500 epochs required for the vanilla DETR, DINO converges at just 36 epochs.
elements.sent-2411-153: Furthermore, plain DINO results in a maximum accuracy drop of 0.6, while our additions boost performance significantly (\eg, compare 66.3 and 68.8 versus 53.8 and 57.2).
elements.sent-2411-154: The performance improvement against vanilla DETR is because DINO uses the MultiScale Deformable Attention mechanism described in \cref{eq:multi_deform_attn , which promotes the cross- and intra-scale interaction.}
elements.sent-2411-155: However, DINO has a lower performance than LETR.
elements.sent-2411-156: Therefore, these two operations lead to the model accepting non-line-segment candidates as potential candidates.
elements.sent-2411-157: We test our idea by removing the box scaling.
elements.sent-2411-158: We noticed an improvement of around 7 points for sAP$^{10}$.
elements.sent-2411-159: We gain 3 extra points for sAP$^{10}$ by adding the line scaling technique, reaching a score of $63.4$.
elements.sent-2411-160: By combining the line scaling and the line rotation (our line contrastive denoising technique), we obtain the maximum of $66.3$ in sAP$^{10}$.
elements.sent-2411-161: Based on our experiments, we conclude that our enhancing method and training technique are effective since DINO with LCDN outperforms LETR using a lower-parameter backbone and fewer training epochs.
elements.sent-2411-162: We want to highlight that the input size of the image for all DINO variations was $512\times512$ for both training and testing.
elements.sent-2411-163: On the other hand, vanilla DETR and LETR resize the images following the procedure described in \cref{sec:experiments for training, and they resize the image with the shortest side of at least 1100 pixels for testing.}
elements.figure-2411-009: \columnwidth{!}{ \begin{tabular}{l c ccc} Model Epochs sAP$^{5}$ sAP$^{10}$ sAP$^{15}$ Vanilla DETR 500 - 53.8 57.2 LETR 825 - 65.2 67.1 DINO 36 45.8 53.2 56.7 \quad- box scaling 36 51.7 60.0 63.5 \quad+ line scaling 36 56.5 63.4 66.2 \quad+ line rotation 36 60.7 66.3 68.7 \end{tabular} } \caption{Ablation study of the different components of LCDN. All models uses ResNet50 as backbone except for LETR that uses ResNet101.} \label{tab:dn_components}
elements.sent-2411-164: An important element of our model is the feature maps generated by the backbone.
elements.sent-2411-165: Here, we evaluate the effectiveness of different feature maps and backbones.
elements.sent-2411-166: We report the results in \Cref{tab: ablation_backbone .}
elements.sent-2411-167: All models are trained using 24 epochs.
elements.sent-2411-168: Adding the S1 feature map produces more precise line segments while slowing down our inference time (measured in the number of frames per second (FPS)).
elements.sent-2411-169: Using SwinL as the backbone gives the best results, but slows down the inference speed.
elements.sent-2411-170: For example, the configuration SwinL with 5 feature maps achieves a score of $69.3$ in the sAP$^{10}$ at 10.5 FPS.
elements.figure-2411-010: \columnwidth{!}{ \begin{tabular}{c c ccc c} Backbone Feat. maps sAP$^{5}$ sAP$^{10}$ sAP$^{15}$ FPS \multirow{2}{*}{ResNet50 \cite{resnet}} S1- S5 60.2 65.5 68.1 13.0 S2 - S5 59.5 65.1 67.5 18.5 \multirow{2}{*}{SwinL \cite{liu2021Swin}} S1- S5 63.8 69.3 71.7 10.5 S2 - S5 62.0 67.8 70.3 13.6 \end{tabular}} \caption{Ablation study based on the number of feature maps and different backbones.} \label{tab: ablation_backbone}
elements.sent-2411-171: Most transformer-based algorithms use upsampling techniques to improve their performance.
elements.sent-2411-172: To evaluate the effects of upsampling an image, we train and test our model at different resolutions for 24 epochs.
elements.sent-2411-173: As documented in \Cref{tab:ablation_upsampling , upsampling benefits both CNN- and transformer-based models.}
elements.sent-2411-174: First, we train DT-LSD following popular CNN-based methods by resizing the original image to 512$\times$512.
elements.sent-2411-175: For DT-LSD 512, we obtain the fastest inference time at 13.6 FPS among all DT-LSD variations.
elements.sent-2411-176: Motivated by DETR-based models \cite{detr,deformable-DETR,dab-DETR,dn-DETR,LETR,dino,anchor-DETR , we also apply scale augmentation consisting of resizing the input image so that the shortest side is a minimum of 480 pixels and a maximum of 800 pixels, while the longest side is a maximum of 1333 pixels.}
elements.sent-2411-177: Here, we choose five different testing sizes, 1) 480, the minimum size used for training, 2), 512, the size used for CNN-based line segment detectors, 3) 640, the size use for YOLO detectors, 4) 800, the maximum size used for training, and 5) 1100, LETR's \cite{LETR testing size.}
elements.sent-2411-178: We note that this scaling technique improved our results over 512$\times$512.
elements.sent-2411-179: For example, DT-LSD 480$^\dagger$ produces better results than DT-LSD 512.
elements.sent-2411-180: As the testing size increases, the sAP metric improves while reducing inference speed (as measured by FPS).
elements.sent-2411-181: As a balance between speed and precision, we choose DT-LSD 640$^\dagger$ because it increases sAP$^{10}$ and sAP$^{15}$ by around 2 points, while its FPS is only by 2.9 fps less than DT-LSD 520$^\dagger$.
elements.sent-2411-182: We did not choose DT-LSD 800$^\dagger$ because the 0.4 improvement in sAP does not justify a drop of 2.5 fps in inference performance.
elements.figure-2411-011: \begin{tabular}{c c c cc c} Model Train Test sAP$^{10}$ sAP$^{15}$ FPS Size Size HAWP 512 512 65.7 67.4 - HAWP 832 832 67.7 69.1 - HAWP 832 1088 65.7 67.1 - DT-LSD 512 512 67.8 70.3 13.6 DT-LSD 800$^*$ 480$^\dagger$ 69.0 71.5 12.2 DT-LSD 800$^*$ 520$^\dagger$ 69.5 71.8 11.8 DT-LSD 800$^*$ 640$^\dagger$ 71.7 73.9 8.9 DT-LSD 800$^*$ 800$^\dagger$ 72.3 74.3 6.4 DT-LSD 800$^*$ 1100$^\dagger$ 72.2 74.2 4.7 \end{tabular} \caption{Ablation study on the effects of image upsampling. We used square images. For 800$^*$, we process images with the smaller dimension between 480 and 800. For the test sizes, we use $\mathrm{number}^\dagger$ to refer to the fact that the smaller dimension of the original image is given by $\mathrm{number}$.} \label{tab:ablation_upsampling}
elements.sent-2411-183: We report the results for training DT-LSD with 12, 24, and 36 epochs in \Cref{tab: ablation_epochs .}
elements.sent-2411-184: DT-LSD gets to competitive performance after training with just 12 epochs.
elements.sent-2411-185: At 12 epochs, DT-LSD achieves a sAP$^{10}$ of 68.4.
elements.sent-2411-186: There is very little difference between 24 and 36 epochs.
elements.sent-2411-187: At 36 epochs, we get a minor increase of 0.3, 0.2, and 0.1 in the sAP$^{5}$, sAP$^{10}$, and sAP$^{15}$, respectively.
elements.figure-2411-012: \begin{tabular}{c ccc} Number of Epochs sAP$^{5}$ sAP$^{10}$ sAP$^{15}$ 12 65.2 68.4 69.7 24 66.6 71.7 73.9 36 66.9 71.9 74.0 \end{tabular} \caption{Ablation study of the training schedule. DT-LSD trained using different numbers of epochs.} \label{tab: ablation_epochs}
elements.sent-2411-188: We report results based on pre-training on different datasets in \Cref{tab: ablation_pretrain .}
elements.sent-2411-189: For our experiments, we use 24 epochs.
elements.sent-2411-190: In our first example, the backbone was pre-trained with the ImageNet-22k dataset \cite{imagenet22k .}
elements.sent-2411-191: In our second example, the DINO was pre-trained using the COCO object detection dataset \cite{coco .}
elements.sent-2411-192: From the results, it is clear that it is essential to pre-train the entire network and not just the backbone.
elements.figure-2411-013: \begin{tabular}{c ccc} Pretrained Weights sAP$^{5}$ sAP$^{10}$ sAP$^{15}$ ImageNet-22k 10.8 12.7 15.6 COCO 66.6 71.7 73.9 \end{tabular} \caption{Ablation study for pre-training using different datasets.} \label{tab: ablation_pretrain}
elements.sent-2411-193: We introduced DT-LSD, a transformer-based model for line segment detection.
elements.sent-2411-194: DT-LSD uses cross-scale interactions to speed up convergence and improve results.
elements.sent-2411-195: Our approach uses pre-training on the COCO dataset to learn low-level features.
elements.sent-2411-196: Our extensive experiment showed that end-to-end transformer-based model can surpass CNN-based methods.
elements.sent-2411-197: Additionally, we opened new opportunities for new line segment detection methods that do not require post-processing steps.
elements.sent-2411-198: In future work, we will consider the development of specialized backbones for transformer-based models.
elements.sent-2411-199: Additionally, an important observation from this work is that DT-LSD needs the COCO pre-trained weights to achieve state-of-the-art results.
elements.sent-2411-200: Therefore, we will also focus on the implementation of the network that is trained from scratch.. { \bibliographystyle{ieee_fullname.}}
